title_en: Importance for Students and Available Editions
content_en:
For students of Shafi'i jurisprudence, Tuhfat al-Muhtaj represents the peak of the classical commentarial tradition on the Minhaj at-Talibin. It is typically studied after a thorough grounding in an-Nawawi's Minhaj and one of the intermediate commentaries, and it is most productive when studied alongside Nihayat al-Muhtaj so that the positions of the two great authorities of the later school can be compared. This parallel study is the method used in advanced Shafi'i institutions in Mecca, Medina, and Tarim, and it produces scholars capable of navigating the full range of the tradition.

The work is dense and requires sustained attention. Ibn Hajar's style is compressed, and many of his arguments are presented in brief formulations that assume familiarity with the prior literature. Students who have not read the Rawdat at-Talibin and the Mughni al-Muhtaj will find many references opaque. For those with adequate preparation, however, the density is a virtue: the Tuhfa packs an extraordinary amount of legal analysis into its ten volumes.

A practical approach for students is to read the Tuhfa alongside its standard hashiyas — those of Ibn Qasim al-Abbadi and Ash-Shirwani — which are printed in the margins of the standard edition. These hashiyas explain Ibn Hajar's compressed formulations, trace his sources, and note points of disagreement with ar-Ramli. Students who can read this layered text are effectively studying four levels of the tradition simultaneously: an-Nawawi's Minhaj, Ibn Hajar's commentary, and two generations of super-commentary.

The standard printed edition is the ten-volume set produced by Dar Ihya al-Turath al-Arabi in Beirut, which includes the hashiyas of Ibn Qasim al-Abbadi and Ash-Shirwani and has been widely reprinted. The complete text is available in digital repositories. Students in Southeast Asia often use Indonesian-language guides to the Tuhfa that have been produced by local scholars to facilitate study in that context.
